data.title: Centessa Pharmaceuticals: Smooth Move To New Programs, But Questions Remain
data.description:
I covered Centessa Pharmaceuticals plc (NASDAQ:CNTA) in September, when it was progressing SerpinPC through registrational trials in Hemophilia B. Then, without any major press release, it announced, through its Q3 earnings release, that it is discontinuing that program and focusing on an earlier stage program targeting sleep disorders. Surprisingly, this news of discontinuation didn’t have much of an effect on the stock, so I want to explore why.

Background

Last September, Contessa was developing SerpinPC, a subcutaneously administered biologic inhibitor of a natural anticoagulant enzyme. The target indication was Hemophilia B, caused by deficiency in Factor IX, which leads to prolonged and spontaneous bleeding. This anticoagulant enzyme, called APC, causes already deficient Factor IX to not work properly because it degenerates Prothrombinase, which is needed for the entire clotting process. Long story short, SerpinPC had a valid mechanism of action.

In my earlier coverage, I noted that SerpinPC...

...was able to reduce mean ABR (annualized bleeding rate, a key metric in hemophilia studies) by 93% at the highest tested dose, which was 1.2 mg/kg once every two weeks for 24 weeks.

That was from an open label study. Updated data also saw SerpinPC being able to maintain this level of ABR reduction. The drug was safe and tolerable as well.

This indication has 4000 US patients, and I noted the very large global market. There were a number of approved drugs, most delivered through an IV injection. SerpinPC’s SC pathway gave it an edge, and, coupled with the efficacy data we saw, it was positioned well in the indication.

Last we heard, they were conducting registrational studies for the asset through their subsidiary ApcinteX. As of now, all these studies show as Terminated on the registry due to a “business and strategic decision.”

Now, the reason for this is the approval of Pfizer’s (PFE) Hympavzi in October 2024, which has a similar mechanism of action. As they state in their March 10-K:

The strategic decision was made to prioritize capital towards the development of our growing OX2R agonist franchise based on the strength of the interim Phase 1 data for ORX750, coupled with the outcome of a planned interim analysis of Part 1 of the PRESent-2 study of SerpinPC, which was evaluated in the context of the evolving treatment and market landscape for hemophilia B, including the recent FDA approval of a competing product.

Contessa has a long history of such discontinuations. In 2022, they discontinued their lead asset, and one more clinical stage asset, and a number of other programs. Here’s a list:

  1. [Centessa announced a]…strategic decision to discontinue development of lixivaptan for Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD) including both the Phase 3 ACTION Study and the open-label ALERT Study. …thorough reassessment of the commercial potential… incremental development challenges and associated costs, following a recent observation of alanine aminotransferase (ALT') and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) elevations in one subject in the ALERT Study.
  2. The Company today announced its decision to discontinue development of ZF874 following a recent report of an adverse event (AE) involving elevated liver enzymes (AST/ALT) …ZF874….was in a Phase 1 study for the treatment of AATD.
  3. As part of ongoing portfolio management, the Company has recently decided to discontinue the small molecule EGFR inhibitor discovery program; evaluate strategic options, including potential divestment, for imgatuzumab; and discontinue internal funding for the lead dual-STAT3/5 degrader program.

It is important to be aware of such history because, right now, the company is telling us they have great data from a molecule named ORX750, and they are asking us to believe in this new molecule. Hark back to 2022, and they were asking us to believe in SerpinPC when they had just let go of phase 3 stage lead asset lixivaptan. Why should we believe them again?

ORX750 data

ORX750 is based on the orexin platform, a neuropeptide that is formed in the hypothalamus and plays a major role in sleep and arousal. The same source says:

To keep us awake, these neuropeptides stimulate other neurons to release neurotransmitters that promote alertness, such as dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine. Without enough orexins, the body has a hard time staying awake and alert. People diagnosed with type 1 narcolepsy have an 85% to 95% reduction in the number of neurons that produce orexins. This loss of orexin-producing neurons leads to the symptoms of narcolepsy, including excessive daytime sleepiness, sleep paralysis, hallucinations, and cataplexy.

A number of orexin antagonists have been developed targeting insomnia. However, orexin agonism is a new field of research. Centessa has two orexin agonists,

ORX750 for the treatment of sleep-wake disorders, including NT1, narcolepsy type 2 (NT2) and idiopathic hypersomnia (IH), and ORX142 for the treatment of EDS in select neurological, neurodegenerative, and psychiatric disorders.

The company published interim phase 1 data on September 10, 2024. Data was from acutely sleep-deprived healthy volunteers. Key data:

  • 2.5 mg dose restored normative wakefulness with mean sleep latency of 32 minutes as measured by the Maintenance of Wakefulness Test (MWT)
  • Favorable safety and tolerability profile with no observations of frequently reported on-target adverse events (AEs) associated with other OX2R agonists, visual disturbances or hepatotoxicity as of the data cutoff date 1
  • PK profile supports once-daily dosing.

Positive topline data was posted in January as well, bolstering these results. So far so good, but the narcolepsy space is highly differentiated. Besides the older stimulants like modafinil (Provigil) and armodafinil (Nuvigil), we have the newer ones like Solriamfetol (Sunosi) and pitolisant (Wakix); Serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) like zoloft and prozac; relatively newer medicines like xyrem; or even newer ones like xywav (xyrem with less sodium), and Lumryz; as well as a number of orexin agonists from the pipelines of Takeda, Alkermes and so on.

Also, note that hepatotoxicity has been observed in OX2R agonists. For example, Takeda’s OX2R agonist TAK-994 saw strong efficacy in NT1, “with statistically significant improvement in daytime wakefulness” and reduction of weekly cataplexy rates at all doses compared to placebo. However, the molecule was shelved after hepatotoxic AEs occurred in trials:

Eight patients exceeded predefined alanine transaminase (ALT) and/or aspartate transaminase (AST) thresholds for subject discontinuation, including three cases meeting Hy’s law criteria (ALT/AST abnormalities >3 x upper limit of normal combined with bilirubin >2 x upper limit of normal; two with TAK-994 180 mg and one with 90 mg), indicating drug-induced hepatotoxicity and leading to the early termination of the trial.

It is true that the data analysis also says that these liver toxicities were probably not caused by OX2R activation because “orexin receptors are not expressed on human hepatocytes or on most immune cells.” Thus, as we see in the ORX750 trial, there were no reports of hepatotoxicity. However, concerns will remain until, I would say, approval.

Takeda is a major competitor for Centessa because, besides this failed molecule, it is developing several orexin agonists. One of these, TAK-861, announced positive data in a phase 2b trial last year. Data showed that 861 had “statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvements across primary and all secondary endpoints up to 8 weeks.” It began a phase 3 trial last year. A second molecule, TAK-925 (Danavorexton), also had positive data in related indications. As to safety, here’s data from TAK-861:

No cases of hepatotoxicity or visual disturbances were reported in the Phase 2b trial or in the ongoing LTE study. The most common TEAEs were insomnia, urinary urgency and frequency, and salivary hypersecretion. Most TEAEs were mild to moderate in severity, and most started within 1-2 days of treatment and were transient.

Alkermes also has orexin agonists, including ALKS 2680, which posted positive phase 1 data back in 2023, showing “dose-dependent, significantly improved sleep latency compared to placebo in narcolepsy type 1.” There were no hepatic AEs. Just two days ago, Alkermes initiated a phase 2 study of ALKS 2680 in idiopathic hypersomnia (IH), one of the three clinical stage indications targeted by ORX750.

NT 2 has been a more difficult indication, with Takeda discontinuing development of TAK-861 in the indication. However, we have no proof yet of what is difficult for Takeda not being equally difficult for Centessa. Jazz Pharma also had an orexin agonist called JZP441, but it stopped trials after reports of visual disturbances from patients.

Surely, Centessa has a long way to go to position ORX750 against this kind of competition and these various AEs. On the positive side, ORX750 may have a better dosage and pk profile, according to some analysts. Many analysts are seeing great potential for orexin agonists, but for a small company like Centessa to succeed in the tough neuroscience field against all these big players - especially after it dumped two lead candidates in three years - seems like a stretch.

Financials

CNTA has a market cap of $1.82bn and a cash balance of $482mn. R&D and G&A expenses last quarter were $60.9mn and $13.7mn respectively. Also note:

As a result of the discontinuation of the program, the Company expects to incur a one-time charge in the fourth quarter of 2024 of approximately $33 - $37 million.

A one-time charge of $31.5 million related to the discontinuation was part of the R&D expenses, which usually stay in the lower $30mn range per quarter. Their CFO was ($42.8mn), and with no capex, their FCF is -$42.8mn as well. That gives them a cash runway of 11+ quarters.

Risks

I have discussed the risks of CNTA here. They are running a phase 2a trial in NT1, NT2, and IH, which will produce data this year. If the trial succeeds, this stock, which has remained unfazed by SerpinPC’s discontinuation, may pop.

One reason for the steady movement of the stock could be that SerpinPC’s potential market was much smaller than what people expect in narcolepsy. Another reason is that CNTA managed to announce positive data from ORX750 while SerpinPC was still around, so the move to ORX750 was smooth. However that may be, my negative arguments could be nullified - if even for a while - by positive data.

Bottom Line

I will wait for phase 2 data before taking a call on Centessa Pharmaceuticals plc. If the data is negative, I will have a very bearish view about the company’s prospect. If the data is positive, I may be more positive, but I will still consider the past history of this company, and wonder whether it will not repeat itself.

data.title: Equity Outlook: Finding Silver Linings In Very Cloudy Markets
data.description:
As volatility rises, staying invested is a strategic priority for capturing long-term return potential in a broadening market.

Global equities faced fresh challenges in the first quarter of 2025 amid growing trade-war concerns and developments in artificial intelligence (AI). Bouts of volatility and an increasingly cloudy outlook underscored the importance of focusing on diversification, valuations and company fundamentals.

After a promising start to the year, global equities lost momentum in February and March (Display). The MSCI ACWI Index of global developed- and emerging-market stocks fell by 1.3% in US-dollar terms during the first quarter, as regional returns diverged. Market returns broadened, as evidenced by the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index’s outperformance of the cap-weighted S&P 500.

Two developments fueled volatility. First, the Chinese company DeepSeek unveiled a new AI model in January that undermined confidence in the earnings prospects of the Magnificent Seven US mega-caps. Second, US policy uncertainty rattled markets, in particular, the potential impact of President Donald Trump’s tariffs on economic growth and businesses. After taking office in January, Trump zigzagged but ultimately imposed levies on all imports from China and on select goods from Mexico, Canada and the European Union, all of which announced retaliatory tariffs.

Regional, Sector and Style Trends Shifted

The lack of clarity around trade policy prompted a divergence in regional returns. In a sharp reversal from long-standing trends, European and emerging-market stocks outpaced US equities during the quarter by a wide margin (Display, above). The MSCI Europe ex UK Index gained 10.7% in US-dollar terms. By contrast, US large-caps - the clear market victors in recent years - gave ground, with the S&P 500 down 4.3%.

Continuing a trend that began late last year, the dominance of the US mega-caps waned. DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) cost-efficient model raised questions about future AI spending, triggering a sell-off of the Magnificent Seven that dragged technology stocks down 11.6% (Display). Energy and utilities led sector gains in the quarter, while healthcare stocks rebounded from a weak, 2024. Lower-volatility stocks did well as investors sought safe havens. Value stocks, which have underperformed growth stocks in recent years, also benefited in the broadening market.

The Complex Effects of a Trade War

The policy conundrums that have dominated headlines aren’t going away soon. In particular, the Trump administration’s on-again, off-again approach to tariffs has left import-dependent US firms susceptible to volatile price swings, while exporters around the world also feel the effects. Many companies are struggling to advance investment plans while the tariff regime remains fluid. But uncertainty might ease if a more systematic approach to tariffs takes root, which could quell investor anxiety, despite concern about the economic fallout of a trade war.

Trade strains and geopolitical risk, from hostilities in Ukraine and the Middle East to China-Taiwan tensions, have threatened to rekindle inflation and muddied the interest-rate outlook. During the first quarter, the US Federal Reserve and Bank of Japan held off on monetary easing, while the European Central Bank cut its benchmark rate 50 basis points to 2.5% - near its long-term target.

For economies and companies, the outcomes of a prolonged trade war will be complex. For example, even Trump has acknowledged that the US could face a recession. That said, the US economy isn’t particularly trade-sensitive, and some companies are more tariff-resistant than others, depending on their industry and operational footprint. Some global companies with US operations might even benefit from tariffs. Examples include European and Japanese electronics manufacturers and consumer goods companies with a large US manufacturing presence.

Expanding Investment Horizons

Investors, too, face complicated challenges with policy uncertainty at a near record high while equity markets broaden. We think the time is right to reevaluate style and regional exposures, particularly as many investors became overweight US growth stocks in recent years because of the Magnificent Seven’s prior dominance.

Defensive equities could add breadth and fortitude. By investing in high-quality stocks with stable trading patterns, a defensive strategy can help reduce volatility created by political, technology and macroeconomic risks. Value stocks offer diversification and still trade at deep discounts to growth stocks. While value stocks are perceived as vulnerable to economic cycles, we believe investors can find undervalued stocks with attractive free cash flows and business attributes that can drive earnings, even in a more sluggish economic environment.

Regional diversification deserves attention. European equities may finally be finding their footing, even as the Russia-Ukraine war remains an ongoing risk. Equity valuations in Europe are still attractive versus US peers, and the average European company beat consensus expectations by 3% during fourth-quarter earnings season. There are even signs of a reversal in outflows from European stock funds over the last three years. Selective investors can find European companies with consistent earnings growth and quality businesses that are more resilient to regional macro and geopolitical risks.

Emerging markets are also showing signs of life and offer hidden opportunities. Some of the biggest players in the global artificial intelligence (AI) supply chain are based in emerging markets. Earnings estimates are trending upward across the developing world, which accounts for 90% of the world’s population and roughly half of global GDP. And our research shows that missing out on an EM recovery could be costly for investors.

Is a Realignment of Earnings Underway?

Recent shifts in equity return patterns call for a closer look at longer-term earnings trends. Over the last 15 years, US corporate earnings growth has outpaced that of non-US companies, represented by the MSCI EAFE Index. However, before 2010, that wasn’t always the case. In fact, our research shows that in three of the four decades since 1970, non-US earnings exceeded US earnings (Display).

This year, US corporate earnings growth is expected to come closer in line with earnings growth of the rest of the world. Meanwhile, equity valuations outside the US are still at a meaningful discount, based on 2025 forecast earnings (Display).

To be sure, even after a tough quarter, we think US stocks remain integral to any diversified allocation. For the last 60 years, US earnings growth has consistently risen, overcoming major economic and geopolitical shocks. Long-standing US advantages—from innovation to education to corporate culture—remain a potent force for equity returns. But how you access those returns matters. As we see it, a US allocation with disciplined active portfolios across the style spectrum is the right way to tap into a changing market.

Sharpening Focus on the “Magnificent Others”

Steep first-quarter declines of the Magnificent Seven have reminded us of the risks of concentrated positions. While the mega-caps include world-class companies at the forefront of the AI revolution, even great companies can pose investing risk. We believe each name should be held in accordance with a portfolio’s philosophy and at appropriate weights, as disruptors themselves can be disrupted.

In the first quarter, DeepSeek demonstrated AI’s disruptive effects on companies and portfolios. As the long-term effects of this technology evolve, we think investors should evaluate opportunities as they do other companies—by doing research to uncover sustainable business models and long-term profit potential. And we continue to see attractive opportunities among what we call the Magnificent Others—high-quality businesses scattered across sectors and styles that have sound balance sheets and consistent earnings streams.

Zooming Out on Volatility: Perspective Matters

Of course, diversified portfolios aren’t immune to volatility. Yet we think some perspective can help investors cope with turbulent markets, after the S&P 500’s peak-to-trough decline of 10.1% in the first quarter of 2025.

In any given calendar year, US stock investors can expect to see sizable negative moves. In fact, since 1980 the S&P has experienced an intra-year peak-to-trough drawdown of 14%, on average. Despite market disruptions—from economic crises to a once-in-a-generation pandemic—the S&P 500 finished higher in 36 of the past 44 years (Display).

US Stocks Have Risen in Most Years Despite Intra-Year Corrections

The past quarter has been instructive on many fronts. We’ve seen that valuations do matter, as shares of the expensive mega-caps pulled back. Another lesson: consensus views can be dramatically wrong, such as the conventional wisdom at the beginning of the year that Trump’s policies would deliver an unequivocal win for US businesses and stocks.

In this changing environment, maintaining a diverse geographic portfolio can help cast a wider net for earnings growth—which can also help investors cope with inflation. Sticky inflation means our assets must work harder to generate returns, and inflation typically leads to higher nominal earnings, which drive stock market returns. That’s why equities have a long history of delivering positive real returns, above the inflation rate.

We’ve also been reminded that volatility is part and parcel of long-term equity investing - and diversification is one of the best antidotes. Investors should gird for more market turbulence and policy uncertainty, particularly around the pace, level and industries affected by tariffs. But there are silver linings in this year’s cloudy market: a less-concentrated equity backdrop that could reward patient equity investors who remain focused on finding companies with strong fundamentals, and more attractive valuations for those who stay invested to achieve their long-term goals.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BioMarin Pharmaceutical: Pegasus Success May Lead To Palynziq Label Expansion
data.description:
BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc. (NASDAQ:BMRN) just the other day announced that it had achieved positive results from its phase 3 Pegasus trial using its drug Palynziq [pegvaliase] to treat patients with phenylketonuria [PKU]. Such positive data was achieved in this late-stage study targeting adolescent patients ages 12 - 17 with PKU specifically. The reason this drug has already been approved to treat adults with this disorder. The reason I believe it is important to go over this company now is because the release of this data brings about several upcoming catalysts for investors to look forward to.

In essence, it is expected to file regulatory applications of Palynziq for the treatment of these adolescent PKU patients to both the FDA and European Medicines Agency [EMA]. Besides these events to track, the company also expects to submit detailed results from this phase 3 Pegasus study at an upcoming medical presentation in 2025. The beauty of this company is that it just doesn't have the advancement of this drug in its pipeline. Another clinical candidate to track from its pipeline would be the development of its next-generation Oligonucleotide for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD], known as BMN 351.

The reason investors should care about this candidate is that BioMarin is set to release initial proof-of-concept data at a Scientific Congress in the 2nd half of 2025. This is likely to be highly scrutinized because it is going to show muscle dystrophin levels established at 25 weeks of dosing. There is no question that the data released then will be compared to that of already approved drugs from Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc. (SRPT). With label expansion of Palynziq for the treatment of patients with PKU now possible later this year, plus the release of 25-week dosing DMD treatment data in the 2nd half of 2025, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Label Expansion Of Palynziq Now Possible With Latest Positive Pegasus Data

As I noted directly above in the beginning, BioMarin was able to report positive data from its phase 3 Pegasus study, which used its drug Palynziq to treat adolescent patients ages 12 - 17 with PKU. Before going over the positive data released from this late-stage study, plus any catalysts to be released from this specific drug development program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for it could end up being.

Phenylketonuria [PKU] is an inherited disorder, whereby an amino acid, known as phenylalanine, builds up in the patient's body. This toxic build-up of this amino acid in the body leads to serious health problems for the patient in question. This is extenuated even further when the person in question either consumes protein or aspartame. The reason it occurs is that there is a change in the phenylalanine hydroxylase [PAH] gene. Why is this a very bad thing? That's because with this change in the gene, there can't be the proper formation of this enzyme needed to break down the amino acid phenylalanine in these patients.

The global phenylketonuria market size is estimated to reach $2.02 billion by 2032. This is a very large market opportunity indeed. Thanks to the positive data achieved in the Pegasus study, the company will be able to expand its label of Palynziq in both the United States and the European Union to treat adolescents ages 12 - 17 with PKU.

In order to see whether or not the company would be in a position to expand its label of Palynziq to treat adolescents with PKU, it ran the phase 3 Pegasus trial. This late-stage study recruited up to a total of 55 patients who were randomized 2:1 to receive either of the following treatment regimens:

  • Pegvaliase [Palynziq].
  • Diet only [Control arm].

One important thing to note is that this particular open-label study had the control arm patients have a "diet only" option to be done for 72 weeks. From there, then patients move to take Palynziq from Week 73 - Week 215. In essence, the initial portion is to see if this drug is capable of establishing a superior reduction of blood Phe levels compared to that of diet alone.

It was reported by the company itself that it indeed was able to meet the primary endpoint of this study, in that treatment with Palynziq resulted in a statistically significant reduction of blood Phe levels compared to diet alone. One item to point out immediately is that this company is in a unique position with this drug. That's because Palynziq is the first and only enzyme therapy to be approved to treat adults with PKU. This means now it can expand its label to include adolescent patients who are between the ages of 12 - 17.

One downside to the data is that no detailed data has yet been revealed, only that the primary endpoint was met with statistical significance. With that said, this brings about one of the three catalysts I noted above in the beginning, which is that the company expects to release detailed data from this phase 3 Pegasus study using Palynziq to treat these PKU patients ages 12 - 17 at an upcoming medical congress later this year in 2025. From there, the other two catalysts would be regulatory filings of this drug to treat this specific PKU patient population in the 2nd half of 2025. The point is that it will be able to expand its label, which in my opinion should be able to expand the market that it can go after.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, BioMarin Pharmaceutical had cash, cash equivalents, and investments of $1.65 billion as of December 31st, 2024. The thing is that this company already has several approved drugs in its pipeline to treat various disorders. Palynziq for the treatment of adults with PKU is one of them and the hope is that the company will be able to expand its label to include adolescent patients ages 12 - 17 with PKU in both the U.S. and European territories. Another drug from its pipeline would be Voxzogo, where for the 12 months ending December 31, 2024, the company was able to generate $735.1 million in revenues for it. It is the only FDA-approved treatment for children with Achondroplasia.

Other drugs that it is currently generating revenue from are: Vimizim, Naglazyme, Aldurazyme, Roctavian, and a few others. It is continuing to guide double-digit revenue growth in 2025 for its company. It makes sense because full-year 2024 revenues grew by a considerable margin. Consider that full-year 2024 total revenues were $2.85 billion, which was a year-over-year increase of 18% [+22% year-over-year when you account for a constant currency factor]. The last item to point out is that it believes that its cash on hand will be enough for a cash runway to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in BioMarin Pharmaceutical. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the recent positive data from the phase 3 Pegasus study, which used Palynziq for the treatment of adolescent PKU patients ages 12 - 17. The goal of the company is to be able to expand its label in both the U.S. and European territories of Palynziq for these adolescents. The risk here is that even if regulatory applications are submitted to both the FDA and EMA, there is no assurance that the agencies will accept them for review for starters. Secondly, even if they are accepted for review, there is no assurance that label expansions of Palynziq will be granted for one or both of these territories.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing phase 1/2 study, which is using next-generation BMN 351 for the treatment of patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD]. It is expected that the company will present 25-week muscle dystrophin production data at a medical congress in the latter part of this year. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the data to be released will be positive, nor viewed positively by the stock market. Not only that but the data to be released will be compared to that of other DMD drugs either approved or in clinical development.

The third and final risk to consider would be the ongoing Canopy clinical program that is ongoing. That's because Voxzogo, under this program, is being evaluated to treat other types of disorders like: Idiopathic short stature, hypochondroplasia [milder form of achondroplasia], SHOX deficiency, Noonan Syndrome and Turner Syndrome. The risk here is that even though Voxzogo worked out well in being able to treat patients with Achondroplasia, there is no assurance that it will perform well in treating patients with hypochondroplasia, nor any of the other disorders I just noted.

Conclusion

BioMarin Pharmaceutical is continuing to do well in terms of revenue growth from its approved products. This was evidenced above by the mere fact that it was able to achieve full-year 2024 total revenues of $2.85 billion. This ended up being a year-over-year increase of 18%. I believe that it is going to continue to achieve an increase in revenues for its regulatory-approved products. Not only because of the revenue it achieved, which I have shown above, but for the fact that it guided higher. It believes that it is set to achieve double-digit revenue and profitability growth in 2025.

There are plenty of catalysts for investors to consider during this year, which may boost shareholder value. It is set to possibly expand the label of Palynziq to include PKU adolescent patients ages 12 - 17. Plus, it is in line to report data from its phase 1/2 study using BMN 351 for the targeting of Exon 51 skipping DMD patients, in the latter part of this year.

data.title: Citigroup Is Still A Mess: But Maybe That's The Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I have mixed feelings after analyzing Citigroup Inc. (NYSE:C). On one hand, the market is moody right now, and most likely, Citi stock will continue to fall at least until the economic situation in the US stabilizes. On the other hand, if you are planning to hold long, and if you believe that Jane Fraser’s restructuring will be successful, this might be a nice opportunity to initiate a position.

There are a couple of reasons why I have mixed feelings: the sentiment is pretty weak, the market is bearish, and people are panicking as a result of new tariffs from President Trump. Besides, Citi remains a weak player in the banking sector. The turnaround is still a work in progress, so we can’t be sure whether it will work. However, the company is at under 0.8x tangible book, has a positive EPS momentum, and the business seems to have become more structured. Predicting the lowest price for the stock is almost impossible, but if you are ready to play a long game, go ahead and BUY Citigroup.

Company Overview

Citigroup is one of the biggest and most famous banks in the world, specializing in investment banking, trading, consumer banking, and wealth management. Even though Citi is one of the biggest names in banking, the stock of this company has been historically trading lower than the stocks of its peers, and it’s not surprising. Citi has been constantly underperforming its competitors, had regulatory troubles, and in general, its business structure was considered inefficient.

Jane Fraser, who began to operate as Citi’s CEO in March 2021, recognized the issues and decided to make this full-scale restructuring in order to simplify the company, which should make it more efficient and competitive. One of the biggest decisions was to exit some non-core international consumer markets, nine out of fourteen to be precise, and to spin off Banamex in Mexico (one of the largest banks in Mexico that Citi acquired in 2001, tried to sell in 2022, but in the end decided to spin it off).

Besides, the company eliminated some parts of its business and continued operating in others, dividing them into five core segments: Services (Treasury and Trade Solutions (TTS), which are cash management solutions for other businesses), Markets (helps businesses, governments, and large investors to buy and to sell financial products on the stock market and to create different financial strategies), Banking, Wealth (financial advisory and planning for high-net-worth individuals) and USPB (US personal banking for individuals).

The goal of all of these changes is to make the business competitive again, and to allow investors to easily track the growth of each segment. This can boost the number of investors interested in this stock. Besides, in my opinion, these changes will help the management to analyze and adjust different parts of its business when something isn’t going as planned.

I liked the fact that they adjusted the return on tangible common equity (RoTCE) prediction in the recent FORM DEF 14A filing. The management lowered expectations to a more realistic 10-11% from 11-12%, which were made in January 2025. In my opinion, it’s not only not a big deal, it’s actually something that makes me trust the management of this company more. I believe that they aren’t simply throwing promises to investors. I think they seriously aim to deliver or even beat these expectations. Besides, the management emphasized the fact that they are working toward long-term stability and not fast, short-term results, which is perfect in the case of such a big enterprise.

Valuation: Cheap, but Justified?

Regarding the return on equity - this is the main metric that indicates how well the company uses its physical assets to generate profits. This is the metric we want to consider when measuring the profitability of a bank. Citi’s return on equity in 2024 was just 6.1%, far below JPMorgan’s 17.2%, Morgan Stanley’s 13.1%, or even Wells Fargo’s 10.8%. But, if we consider that the management aims to reach 10-11% in 2026, it seems they want to be in line with the competitors or maybe even better than some. By the way, this prediction is for 2026 and not for 2025 because this is when, I believe, the management expects the investments and transformation to take effect. This is the main reason why I wouldn’t touch the stock of this company if I hoped for quick profits. One year from now is the minimum amount of time the company will need to show some results (and, of course, nothing is guaranteed).

Citigroup is the most undervalued bank in terms of tangible book value. It trades at just 0.79x, and it’s more than 40% lower than the sector median. This level suggests that investors still don’t trust Citi’s ability to show some strong returns. When you compare that to JPMorgan, which trades at 2.6x, or Morgan Stanley at 2.59x, it’s clear that something is off. Or at least still off until the company fixes its issues.

Earnings per share grew 47% year-over-year in 2024, while forward EPS growth is expected to be at 32%. Both are very strong numbers compared to the sector. Long-term EPS growth is projected to be at around 23%, which is still more than twice better than the sector's median. It means that the company has a bigger net income compared to the number of shares outstanding, or strong profitability, and a higher potential to allocate its earnings to shareholders through dividends or reinvestment back into the business.

The valuation is incredibly low, but the PEG is especially impressive. The forward PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth) is just 0.4, compared to the sector’s median of 1.1. It means that the market does not believe that Citi will show any significant growth in the future. If management continues to deliver, and if profitability improves slightly, this could present a perfect investment opportunity. It's impossible to predict whether this transformation initiative will be successful because it's a very difficult task to do and to manage for such a dinosaur as Citigroup, but… If it is successful, we might see massive returns from this stock in the future.

I must express optimism regarding Jane Fraser's leadership. I think that changes in these old financial institutions are long overdue. I am impressed by her bold decisions, the extent of her modernization plans, and her up-to-date approach. I read that she wants to continue with a hybrid working model, which is not very common for financial institutions. Even big tech enterprises, which are supposed to be the pioneers of the new and free working manner, are trying to play micromanagers and get everyone back to offices. It appears Jane Fraser thinks that this will be an advantage of working for Citigroup, which I 100% agree with. It allows the company to bring on the best people from on-site working model competitors like JPMorgan.

So, for the conclusion of this section - compared to other huge banks, Citi is not performing well. It is behind in terms of return on equity but trades at the lowest multiple relative to tangible book value, which could present an opportunity for long-term investors. That alone doesn’t make it a buy, but it gives you a framework. You’re not buying Citi because it’s the best bank. You’re buying it because it’s very cheap and has room to grow if the transformation succeeds. That’s the whole thesis. If you don’t believe in this transformation, you have no reason to invest, not in the short-term and not in the long term.

Economic Risks and Opportunities

From a macro perspective, Citi is in a weird place. On the one hand, if rates are stable, it could really help the bank, especially in the U.S. retail side where higher rates usually mean better margins. Their Services and Wealth businesses are also solid and could keep bringing in reliable, fee-based income. If the restructuring actually delivers, I don’t expect Citi to suddenly turn into JPMorgan, but it could finally start to close the gap a little, and that alone would be a win.

That said, the risks are still very real. Citi was fined again in 2024 "for failing to make sufficient progress in remediating problems with data quality management" - and this is the continuation of 2020 consent orders against the bank. Cash from operations is at negative $19.6 billion, which is a huge red flag even if you believe in the turnaround, although JPMorgan is at negative $42 billion.

The recession brings in a full load of different risks. For example, in a recession, people and businesses struggle to pay back loans. That means more defaults, especially in credit cards and consumer lending, which is a big part of Citi’s USPB segment. Another factor to consider is that if the Fed starts to cut rates aggressively to fight a recession, Citi will earn less from the difference between what it pays on deposits and what it earns from lending (that’s net interest income - NII). On the other hand, if inflation grows as a result of tariffs (and it most likely will), and if the Fed decides to raise the rates, the NII of Citi could go up. So it’s half risk and half opportunity, depending on where the situation takes us. The banking segment could suffer as well in a recession due to fewer IPOs, M&As, etc.

Conclusion

Citigroup is not a good company for those who cannot wait for as long as will be needed. It’s far away from being fully “fixed” and far away from being a leader in US banking, although the potential is there. The management works on strategy, structure, and improving performance. The stock is cheap for a reason, but that doesn’t mean it has to stay that way. If you believe in Jane Fraser’s plan, and you’re willing to hold through these uncertainties, this could become one of the best long-term value plays in large-cap finance. As a result, I rate Citigroup as a BUY for long-term investors and as a HOLD from entering for those who want a quick profit or are skeptical about Jane Fraser’s transformation.

For those who decided to buy, just know, it’s going to take time. And a bit of faith.

data.title: Absci Corporation: A Potential Turnaround Play In Biotech Amid A Tumultuous Market
data.description:
What Happened to Absci Stock

Absci Corporation (NASDAQ:ABSI) continues to lose investor confidence. Shares have dropped significantly since February 2025 and are now trading just above $2. This decline has taken the stock close to its 52-week low.

We believe the latest earnings report likely contributed to this weakness. Absci reported revenue of only $0.7 million in Q4, which missed consensus estimates. The company also posted a wider-than-expected loss per share. Shares dropped more than 7% after the report was released.

Even before earnings, the stock had started to give up its February 2025 gains. In January, Absci announced a strategic partnership and equity investment from Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD). The stock jumped over 40% on the news but gave back all of those gains within weeks. That reversal suggests that sentiment has not shifted in a meaningful way, in our opinion.

We think broader sector dynamics are also playing a role. Early-stage biotech stocks have remained under pressure. Risk-off sentiment, a higher cost of capital, and ongoing capital scarcity have weighed on the entire group. For a company like Absci, which generates almost no revenue and burns cash, that backdrop may be especially difficult.

ABSI's Financials Remain Weak, Though Liquidity Has Improved

Absci reported a full-year 2024 revenue of $4.5 million. That was down from $5.7 million in 2023. We believe this drop suggests that partnered revenue has not grown in a meaningful way. That is not an encouraging trend for a company that has signed deals with large pharmaceutical partners.

At the same time, research and development spending has increased. Absci spent $63.9 million on R&D in 2024. That was a 33% increase year over year. Management has explained that this increase is tied to internal pipeline development. Absci is advancing at least two programs into clinical trials. While the investment may make sense strategically, it also increases the burn rate.

Net loss for the year was $103.1 million. That was a slight improvement from the $110.6 million loss posted in 2023, but the company still consumed $72 million in cash during the year.

Cash and equivalents at year-end totaled $112.4 million. That was boosted further by the private placement with AMD. Management has reiterated that the company is now funded into the first half of 2027. We think that projection may be overly optimistic. It assumes continued cost discipline and stable investment across programs. If development timelines change or if new programs are advanced more quickly, the runway could shorten. Still, we think the added capital has removed near-term dilution risk, and that may become important if sentiment turns.

Internal Programs Are the New Focus

Absci is shifting focus toward internal pipeline development. Its lead program, ABS-101, could enter clinical trials in the first half of 2025. This candidate targets TL1A, a cytokine tied to inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). TL1A inhibition has gained attention recently, and we think interest in the space has increased following clinical progress from competitors like Prometheus (acquired by Merck) and Roivant. If Absci’s molecule shows encouraging safety and early signals of activity, it could support both the TL1A target and Absci’s design approach. That said, we believe expectations remain low at this stage.

ABS-201 is aimed at androgenic alopecia, a condition that affects an estimated 80 million people in the U.S. The market for hair loss treatments hit roughly $2.7 billion in 2023 and is growing at a rapid pace of a CAGR of 8.45% through 2028. Most current options, like minoxidil and finasteride, deliver only modest results and are often linked to hormonal side effects or poor long-term adherence. In our view, if Absci’s approach can demonstrate even incremental improvement, it could gain traction in a market with significant unmet demand. Absci is targeting the prolactin receptor, which may regulate follicle cycling. If the science is promising in early studies, we think this could offer a novel, hormone-sparing approach that fills a large unmet need.

It is also worth noting that aside from ABS-101 and ABS-201, Absci is developing:​

  • ABS-301: A potential first-in-class antibody targeting an undisclosed immuno-oncology marker, with potential in squamous cell carcinomas and other indications. Absci anticipates selecting a drug candidate in the first half of 2025. ​

  • ABS-501: An AI-designed anti-HER2 antibody with novel epitope interactions and increased or equivalent affinity to trastuzumab in preclinical settings (for more information, see Absci's pipeline breakdown on its website). The candidate has shown efficacy against trastuzumab-resistant tumors expressing wild-type HER2. Absci plans to select a drug candidate in 2025.

These programs represent a major shift from Absci’s earlier focus on partner-only R&D. This internal pipeline adds upside potential, but also raises burn rates and brings more development risk. In our view, these programs are likely not reflected in the current valuation, and the announcement of drug candidates for ABS-301 and ABS-501 may serve as a significant catalyst that investors should look out for. The stock seems dirt cheap in light of all this. If any of these programs succeed in early trials, it could significantly change investor perception and propel shares higher. But, we also recognize the possibility of failure, and so we believe investors should remain realistic about timelines and risk.

The Platform Model Still Matters

Absci’s business has long centered on its Integrated Drug Creation platform. This combines generative AI and synthetic biology to design new antibody drug candidates that may otherwise take years to develop. The idea is to design binders from scratch (de novo) using AI, and then validate them in lab settings. Absci’s pitch is that this combination of wet-lab testing and computational design can deliver drugs faster and with more precision than traditional screening.

We believe this platform still plays a central role in Absci’s story. It is the foundation for both its internal programs and its partnerships. In theory, a successful demonstration of its speed or success in drug design could make the platform a more widely used tool across pharma.

Partnership Activity Continues

Absci is not abandoning its platform model. In fact, the company has continued to sign new collaborations. A deal with AstraZeneca PLC (AZN) announced in late 2023 may be worth up to $247 million in potential milestones. Absci has also collaborated with Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) and other major players.

The AMD partnership, announced in January 2025, is intended to accelerate Absci’s AI model training by leveraging AMD’s compute infrastructure. AMD also took an equity stake, which we believe adds further credibility. We encourage readers to also consult a prior article for more background on the AMD partnership with the company.

In our opinion, these collaborations show that Absci’s approach is still drawing interest from large and well-capitalized players. While revenue from these deals remains minimal, we believe new partnerships are still possible and could act as significant and surprise catalysts. Given the big pharma attention the company has already received, we think it is reasonable to expect new partnerships to be announced in the near future. A new deal, especially one that includes upfront funding or validates Absci’s platform further, could lift sentiment and, in turn, the stock price.

Sentiment Has Turned Sharply Negative

The biotech sector has struggled broadly and the massive decline in the overall market on April 3, 2025, certainly has not helped in lifting sentiment around Absci. Ultimately, we think Absci’s valuation may not reflect its own progress as much as it reflects overall sentiment.

Looking at institutional interest, Cathie Wood's ARK Genomic Revolution ETF has continued to hold 10,442,325 shares as of April 2, 2025 (the fund's 14th largest holding). Perhaps more telling is the fact that the fund picked up another 437,000 shares on March 19, 2025. While such does not necessarily validate the company's pipeline, as it is still in very early stages, we think these investments may suggest that "smart money" still believes in the company's story.

Conclusion: High Risk, But Some Pieces Are Coming Together

Absci is still in a very early stage. In our opinion, the company has not generated meaningful revenue and may not do so for some time. But it does now have a real pipeline, some well-known partners, and enough cash to get through the next two years.

The stock price has collapsed as of late, and the business model still has much to prove. But there has been progress. For investors with a high-risk tolerance, we believe the current valuation may offer an entry point.

In our opinion, the risk remains high, and execution will be critical. There are a lot of things that could go wrong - namely, the failure of its internal programs. We could also see a decline in interest in the company's platform model, with no new partnerships and the loss of existing ones. Cash burn is also an issue, and that issue certainly is not expected to let up with the advancement of its internal programs in the clinic. But, we still think the crucial pieces are there in order to invest in the stock. Ultimately, we think the current price reflects more fear than fact, and that the upside outweighs the downside at this level.

data.title: Nu Holdings: Where Value Investors and Growth Hunters Align
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Cathie Wood and Warren Buffett could not be more different in their investing style, but they have one thing in common: Nubank (NYSE:NU)

Nubank is one of the few stocks that is owned by both of these investors, and it's not hard to see why.

Nubank offers a combination of growth and value, especially at today's discounted price.

Though the company has suffered some minor setbacks, it is incredibly well-positioned to benefit from fintech growth in Latin America-

I rate Nu a Strong Buy, highlighting potential buy areas based on technical analysis.

What's Been Happening With Nu?

Before we get into the fundamentals, it's important to look at what's been going on with Nubank.

After enjoying a pretty uninterrupted bullish run in 2023 and 2024, Nubank is now down over 40% from its all-time high.

Yes, we can attribute some of this to market sentiment, but there's something else going on.

There's been a notable increase in "bad loans" or non-performing loans, NPL, at Nubank over the last year.

As we can see, this peak is in the third quarter of 2024, and the number has fallen since.

This has also been accompanied by notable volatility and depreciation in the Brazilian real, which could have contributed to some volatility.

But with Nubank looking to solve its NPL problem and the Brazilian real now stabilising, this could be the perfect time to add.

The Worst May Now Be Behind Us

First off, it's important to address these two concerns.

On the matter of NFP, we've already seen some encouraging improvements in the last quarter.

I believe Nu has taken this early lesson and is now applying it to its new products, like Credit Cards, where it outperforms its peers.

Ultimately, Nu has felt some growing pains, but it's definitely not too late to change things. The next quarter will be crucial in determining if what we have here is an improved trend in NPL.

On the currency side, I'd say it's now a great time to gain exposure to the Brazilian real. The dollar is in free fall as I write this, owing to Trump's tariff talk, and this means that Nu's earnings, priced in dollars, will be noticeably higher next quarter.

Overall, not a bad idea now to gain some global diversification and Nu provides this.

Cathie And Buffett Agree: Value And Growth

Nu offers the best of both worlds: growth and a fair valuation, and I believe that's why it's attracted both Cathie and Warren.

Cathie Wood recently increased her positions in Nu across her funds following Q4 results, and it's clear she believes in the growth story.

She's not the only one, with consensus analyst estimates forecasting over 25% YoY growth and almost 30% growth in EPS.

Nu has carved itself a great spot in the banking sector in Brazil. It may take longer than expected, and profits may fluctuate, but there's no doubt that LatAm is in need of financial services, with perhaps ¼ of the population still unbanked.

And Nu also offers value, especially after this sell-off.

What's interesting is that Buffett was an early investor in NU, buying shares back in 2021. He actually sold around ⅕ of his position in the last two quarters. He actually sold quite near the top, perhaps thinking valuation was frothy, or perhaps simply because he was raising cash.

But would Buffett be buying back at these prices?

With a PE of 26 and a PEG of 0.29, I'd argue NU is undervalued considering its incredible revenue and EPS growth potential.

This stock ticks all the boxes in my opinion.

Technical Analysis

Nu is fast approaching some very significant support, and this gives us a great entry opportunity.

The stock is, in my opinion, forming an ABC retracement in a wave 4. Baked on Fibonacci retracement of the last rally, wave 3, this could land us somewhere between the 50% and 61.8% levels. That's between $9.75 and $8.25.

At around $8 we also have a tonne of volume support, and we can also see the weekly RSI is getting quite close to oversold.

In my opinion, this is a good spot to add, but I might leave some dry powder to buy more near the next fib level at $8.25.

Risks

Nu is still a young company, operating in a volatile geographical area. Politics and FX risks are ever present, and investors sometimes shy away from these types of stocks.

If we see a global slowdown, this could derail Nu, which has been aggressively expanding its portfolio, and would likely see a big increase in loan defaults.

Final Thoughts

All in all, Nu offers value and growth, as well as global diversification. This seems like a great spot to buy after such a big sell-off, with strong support not far below us.

data.title: Bad Stock Picking Exacerbates Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund's High Fees
data.description:
Picking good stocks is hard, which is why many investors are drawn to ETFs and mutual funds.

The problem with that strategy is that it assumes fund managers pick good stocks when, for decades, we’ve known that 75%+ funds underperform their benchmarks every year. Every year!

So, how can you gain some insight into whether or not a fund will outperform? Look at past price performance? No. How about analyzing each of the holdings of a fund? Yes!

That’s exactly what we do for our forward-looking Fund Ratings for 7,600+ ETFs and mutual funds. After scouring our coverage universe, we found a mid-cap mutual fund that allocates too much capital to bad stocks. To make matters worse, it charges well above-average fees for its below average (compared to the benchmark and overall market) portfolio. Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund (MUTF:SMDVX) is this week’s Danger Zone pick.

Forward-Looking Research Protects Investors

Most legacy fund research is backward-looking. It is based on past price performance. Our fund research is forward-looking and based on fundamental analysis and ratings on each individual holding.

SMDVX earns our Very Unattractive (equivalent to Morningstar’s 1-Star) rating, while Morningstar gives SMDVX a 4-Star rating. Figure 1 shows how our forward-looking Fund Ratings compare to Morningstar’s (MORN) ratings.

SMDIX and SMDRX, two other share classes of the mutual fund, also earn a 4-Star rating, while we rate them Unattractive. HFDRX, HFDSX, HFDTX, HFDYX, HFDFX earn a 3-Star rating, and we rate all of them Unattractive. HFDCX, which we rate Unattractive, is the only share class that earns a 2-Star rating.

Figure 1: Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund Ratings

Sources: New Constructs, LLC, company filings, mutual fund filings, and Morningstar

Hard to Trust a Methodology that Lacks Transparency

Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund looks for three types of companies with distinct objectives: capital growth, lower downside volatility, and idiosyncratic growth.

The fund’s prospectus goes on to note the fund’s sub-adviser uses “bottom-up fundamental analysis to select securities.” More specifically, the fund’s sub-advisor seeks to identify securities that offer the potential upside based on:

  • novel, superior, or niche products or services,
  • sound operating characteristics,
  • quality of management
  • an entrepreneurial management team,
  • opportunities provided by mergers, divestitures, or new management,
  • or other factors.

However, details on how the sub-adviser calculates or quantifies any of the items above is absent. Without such information, we can only judge SMDVX’s managers based on what we can measure: the quality of the stocks they hold in their fund.

Our analysis reveals SMDVX’s investment process leads to holdings with lower returns on invested capital (ROIC), lower cash flows, and more expensive valuations than its benchmark and the S&P 500. See below.

Holdings Research Reveals A Low-Quality Mid-Cap Portfolio

Our holdings analysis, which leverages our Robo-Analyst technology, reveals that SMDVX holds lower-quality stocks than its benchmark iShares Russell Mid-Cap ETF (IWR). For reference, IWR earns a Neutral rating.

Per Figure 2, SMDVX allocates 57% of its portfolio to Unattractive-or-worse rated stocks, compared to 45% for IWR. On the flip side, SMDVX allocates only 9% of its assets to Attractive-or-better rated stocks, compared to 14% for IWR.

Figure 2: Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund Allocates to Far Worse Stocks than IWR

Sources: New Constructs, LLC, company, ETF, and mutual fund filings

Per Figure 3, our holdings analysis also reveals SMDVX’s portfolio is lower quality than the S&P 500, as represented by State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which earns an Attractive rating.

At just 33% of its portfolio, SPY allocates less to Unattractive-or-worse rated stocks compared to SMDVX.

Figure 3: Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund Allocates to Worse Stocks than SPY

Sources: New Constructs, LLC, company, ETF, and mutual fund filings

Given SMDVX allocates just 9% of assets to Attractive-or-better rated stocks, it appears poorly positioned to generate the outperformance required to justify higher active management fees.

Expensive Stocks Drive Very Unattractive Risk/Reward Rating

Figure 4 shows our detailed rating for SMDVX, which includes each of the criteria we use to rate all ETFs and mutual funds under coverage. These criteria are the same for our Stock Rating Methodology, as the performance of a mutual fund equals the performance of its holdings minus fees. Figure 4 also compares SMDVX’s rating with those of IWR and SPY.

Figure 4: Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund Rating Details

Sources: New Constructs, LLC, company, ETF, and mutual fund filings

As Figure 4 shows, SMDVX is inferior to IWR in four of the five criteria that make up our Portfolio Management rating. Specifically:

  • SMDVX’s ROIC is 10%, which is lower than IWR’s (13%) and SPY’s (33%) ROIC.
  • SMDVX’s 2-yr average free cash flow (FCF) yield of 1% is lower than IWR’s and SPY’s at 2%.
  • The price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio for SMDVX is 5.9, which is greater than the 3.9 for IWR and 3.8 for SPY.
  • Our discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis reveals an average market-implied growth appreciation period (GAP) of 72 years for SMDVX’s holdings compared to 59 years for IWR’s and 72 years for SPY’s holdings.

Market expectations for stocks held by SMDVX imply profits will grow substantially more than the stock’s held by IWR and SPY (measured by PEBV ratio), despite IWR’s and SPY’s holdings being much more profitable (as measured by ROIC).

This rigorous holdings analysis reveals that SMDVX provides exposure to less profitable mid-cap companies, while taking much more valuation risk than the benchmark and S&P 500.

High Fees Make Owning SMDVX Worse

At 3.54%, SMDVX’s total annual costs (TAC) are higher than 95% of Mid Cap Blend mutual funds under coverage. For comparison, the simple average TAC of all the Mid Cap Blend mutual funds under coverage is 1.56% and the asset-weighted average is 1.11%. IWR charges just 0.21% and SPY has total annual costs of just 0.10%. Why pay higher fees for inferior stock selection?

Our TAC metric accounts for more than just the expense ratio. We consider the impact of front-end loads, back-end loads, redemption fees, and transaction costs – the latter of which adds 0.10% to SMDVX’s TAC.

Figure 5 shows our breakdown of SMDVX’s total annual costs, which we provide for all ~6,300+ mutual funds and ~1,300+ ETFs under coverage.

Figure 5: Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund’s Total Annual Costs Breakdown

Sources: New Constructs, LLC, company, ETF, and mutual fund filings

To justify charging higher fees, SMDVX must outperform its benchmark by 332 basis points annually over three years.

However, SMDVX’s load-adjusted 3-year quarter-end average annual total return has underperformed IWR by 590 basis points and SPY by 1,553 basis points. SMDVX has also underperformed over the 1-year, 5-year, and 10-year periods.

Given that 57% of assets are allocated to stocks with Unattractive-or-worse ratings, and 87% are allocated to stocks with Neutral-or-worse ratings, SMDVX is likely to continue underperforming.

Get An Edge From Holdings-Based Fund Analysis

We offer clients in-depth reports for all ~7,600+ ETFs and mutual funds under coverage. Smart fund (or ETF) investing means analyzing each of the holdings of a fund. Failure to do so is a failure to perform proper due diligence. Simply buying an ETF or mutual fund based on past performance does not necessarily lead to outperformance. Only thorough holdings-based research can help determine if a fund’s methodology leads managers to pick high-quality or low-quality stocks.

Easily Make Any Fund, Even SMDVX, Better

New technologies enable investors to create their own fund without any fees, while also enabling better, more sophisticated weighting methodologies. For example, if we reallocate the fund’s capital to the companies with the best Core Earnings, our customized fund allocates:

  • 17% of assets to Attractive-or-better rated stocks (compared to 9% for SMDVX)
  • 37% of assets to Unattractive-or-worse rated stocks (compared to 57% for SMDVX)

Compare the quality of stock allocation in as-is SMDVX vs. our customized version of SMDVX in Figure 6.

Figure 6: Hartford Schroders US MidCap Opportunities Fund Allocation Could Be Improved

Sources: New Constructs, LLC, company, and mutual fund filings

Better Options For Mid-Cap Blend Funds

Below we present Mid Cap Blend ETFs or mutual funds that feature an Attractive rating, over $100 million in assets under management, and below average TAC:

  1. Alpha Architect U.S. Quantitative Value ETF (QVAL) – 0.32% TAC and Very Attractive rating
  2. State Street SPDR Russell 1000 Volatility Focus ETF (ONEV) – 0.22% TAC and Very Attractive rating
  3. Invesco Bloomberg Analyst Rating Improvers ETF (UPGD) – 0.44 TAC and Very Attractive rating
  4. Gotham 1000 Value ETF (GVLU) – 0.55% TAC and Very Attractive rating
  5. Jensen Quality Mid Cap Fund (JNVSX) – 0.96% TAC and Very Attractive rating

This article was originally published on March 10, 2025.

Disclosure: David Trainer, Kyle Guske II, and Hakan Salt receive no compensation to write about any specific stock, sector, style, or theme.

data.title: TCW Transform Supply Chain ETF Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The world’s economies are undergoing a monumental transformation, reshoring from global supply chains to comparatively local systems. North America is a unique beneficiary of this shift, which we view as a major investment opportunity.

During the 75 years after World War II, the world embarked on a path of building supply chains focused on minimizing manufacturing costs, often producing components in distant locations. This approach sacrificed resilience and reliability, a trade-off starkly exposed by disruptions like the COVID-19 pandemic, trade wars and an increase in tariffs, geopolitical conflicts and tensions, and natural disasters around the world. Companies have already begun to relocalize their supply chains to North America. SUPP’s thesis is that this intricate transformation of global supply chains requires trillions of dollars in investment. We see this as an incredible opportunity to invest in the enablers and beneficiaries of this transformation.

Performance

Net of fees, SUPP returned -4.1% in Q4 2024 compared to 2.4% for the S&P 500. For the year ending December 31, 2024, SUPP returned 11.0% while the S&P 500 returned 25.0%. Since its inception in February 2023, SUPP has delivered a cumulative return of 26.7% compared to 46.3% for the S&P 500 over the same period.

While the total portfolio did not keep pace with the major equity market indices, our investments in the semiconductor sector generally performed well. Holdings such as TSMC (TSM) and NVIDIA (NVDA) saw increases of 14.0% and 10.6%, respectively in the quarter, as the companies continue to experience more demand than they are able to supply for their most advanced technologies. The semiconductor value chain sits in the center of national security interests and geopolitical tensions and our investments are seeing the benefits of the continued U.S. focus on building out an independent domestic semiconductor supply chain to ensure semiconductor and computer security. GFL Environmental (GFL) also contributed nicely to performance in the quarter with shares up 11.7% as the company provided additional transparency on its process to sell its Environmental Services segment at an attractive valuation implying a core solid waste business that would be valued well below industry-leading peers.

Investments in companies with direct exposure to light commercial and residential construction were detractors from performance during the quarter. Most of the weakness was a function of rates as the 10-year treasury increased by nearly 80 basis points during the quarter despite the Fed cutting its target rate by 100 basis points since mid-September. Higher rates are generally associated with softer light commercial construction activity and our investments in Advanced Drainage (WMS) and Ferguson (FERG) underperformed as a function of this backdrop. Additionally, our investment in Canadian Pacific (CP) declined by 15.2% following threats of tariffs up to 25% on Mexico which represents a large and growing market for the railroad. We view these dynamics as temporary and continue to have strong conviction in these investments and their role in the supply chain reshoring megatrend.

Why Active Thematic ETFs?

The rise of the “Magnificent Seven”* and tech stocks may lead some investors to believe they are well exposed to thematic growth – but the data tells us otherwise. Only 8% of SUPP’s portfolio holdings are captured by the S&P 500.

Investors are too tethered to indices and as a result underexposed to capturing the opportunities presented by these once-in- a-generation economic transformations. These transformations are complex and developing rapidly. As active managers we can drive the greatest returns from these megatrends by reacting quickly to new insights or when facts change.

Market Commentary

The U.S. stock market, as represented by the S&P 500 Index, delivered an impressive return of 25.0% (total return) through 2024. This positive performance was driven by corporate earnings that have exceeded market expectations and a solid macroeconomic backdrop that seemed to support the Fed’s goal to achieve a “soft landing”.

With fourth quarter earnings season kicking off, the S&P 500 is forecasted to see annual EPS growth of nearly 11% in 2024 (UBS, 01/06/2025), although the top-heavy concentration of the index certainly inflates that figure. The “Magnificent 7”, whose market capitalization consistently made up 30%+ of the S&P 500 in 2024, have buoyed EPS growth, with 2024 estimates at 36.2%, and the rest of the companies in the index (the S&P 493), with a much more modest 4.8% estimate.

Looking forward, earnings growth is expected to accelerate in 2025, while dispersion is expected to improve. In 2025, earnings for the S&P 500, Magnificent 7, and S&P 493 are projected to be 12.6%, 19.7%, and 9.2%, respectively, which we believe to be an attractive set up for us as active investors.

At a macro level, the economy appears to be on solid footing, although this had led to a sharp change in expectations for the Fed to continue its cut cycle. The unemployment rate is still historically low (4.1% in December), while average hourly earnings growth is above the historical average of the last two decades (3.9% in December). At the same time, the Fed’s progress on inflation has seemingly stalled (2.9% in December), and expectations for interest cuts in 2025 fell from ~5 cuts of ~120 bps at the start of the quarter, to ~4 cuts of ~43 bps through the end of the quarter (Bloomberg, 01/21/2025).

Finally, the Trump 2.0 administration is a major question mark for the U.S. and global economies. So far, the U.S. equity market has strongly approved of his victory while international markets have provided a more mixed response. Trump is notoriously tough on China and Mexico, and generally champions any movement that puts “America first”, however, his comments have historically often lacked follow-through. Trump and his cohorts have promised to cut regulations that are seen as “bad for [U.S.] business”, which is perhaps one of the main contributors to post-election performance.

In the present environment, we believe it remains prudent to focus on compelling secular trends which are more insulated from economic growth worries. As always, we reiterate our unswerving focus on all-weather businesses which exhibit proven management skill, pricing power, low levels of indebtedness, strong free cash flow generation, and a likely resilience in the face of a pronounced economic slowdown.
